content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sncramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Dorine Martirosian 
Senior Assistant City Attorney 
613 E. Broadway, Suite 220 
Glendale, CA 91206 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-260 
Dear Ms. Martirosian: 
December 15, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmembers Paula Devine 
and Ara Najarian regarding Government Code Section l 090, et seq., 1 and the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2 Please note that we do not advise on any other 
area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a 
finder of fact when rendering advice (111 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we 
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 5).) 
QUESTION 
Under the Act or Section I 090, do either Councilmember Devine or Councilmember 
Najarian have a conflict of interest due to each member's respective ownership interest in General 
Electric ("GE") stock in regards to the proposal bids and subsequent contract for the City's power 
plant project, where GE is listed as an approved vendor of equipment in the City's request for 
proposals? 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. 
2 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. The regulations 
of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California 
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless 
otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-17-260 
Page No. 2 
No. Under the Act, neither Councilmember has a conflict of interest in participating in the 
decisions. Further, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmembers from making or 
participating in making, or the City from entering into, the contract because both the 
Councilmembers' respective ownership interests in GE stock qualify for the noninterest exception 
set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(I). 
FACTS 
The City of Glendale ("City") is awaiting the receipt of proposals for an Engineering, 
Procurement and Construction Contract ("EPC Contract") for the upgrade and repowering of its 
power plant ("Repower Project"). The overall contract amount for the EPC Contract is anticipated 
to be approximately $200 million. The proposals are due in December. 
The City's Request for Proposals ("RFP") for the EPC Contract included a list of technically 
acceptable suppliers for the major equipment and bulk commodities the City expects the EPC 
Contractor to supply under the contract. In your email of November 20,2017, you provided 
additional information that, "[t]he EPC Contractor's proposal will not identify specific vendors for 
the equipment except that the EPC Contractor must specify if it intends to identify a vendor for the 
emergency generator other than one of the three suppliers of emergency generators listed in the 
RFP." GE is not an approved vendor for the emergency generator. The City project manager's 
permission is required if the EPC Contractor selects a vendor other than one listed in the attachment 
to the RFP. The EPC Contractor may also identify additional equipment needed after the award of 
the EPC Contract, also subject to the City project manager's approval. 
You state that no rights will be created for the vendor under the RFP or the resulting EPC 
Contract. Vendors may be selected by the EPC Contractor after the EPC Contract is awarded. The 
vendor's contract is with the EPC Contractor, and the purchaser will be the EPC Contractor, not the 
City. The EPC Contractor may swap out a vendor without that vendor's permission. 
GE is listed as an approved vendor for some of the categories of equipment in the RFP. 
Each equipment item that lists GE has at least one additional approved vendor. The maximum 
amount of equipment the EPC Contractor could purchase from GE is between $6 to $8 million, or 3 
to 4 percent, of the $200 million total contract price. The highest priced GE equipment is 
approximately $4 million, with the lowest priced GE equipment at or less than $1 million. 
Councilmember Devine owns GE stock valued over $2,000. Neither her annual income 
from GE dividends nor any other payments from GE exceed 5 percent of her total annual income. 
Councilmember Najarian owns GE stock valued over $2,000, and neither his annual income from 
GE dividends nor any other payments from GE, exceed 5 percent of his total annual income. GE is 
a publicly traded, multinational conglomerate corporation with revenues of approximately $120 
billion in 2016, and the value of its outstanding shares is approximately $153.71 billion. Both 
councilmembers own less than 3 percent of the outstanding shares of GE stock. 


ANALYSIS 
The Act 
File No. A-17-260 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official knows, or has reason to know, he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that 
a public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, 
on one or more of the official's financial interests. As applicable to the facts, Section 87103 
identifies the following interests: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth at least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a 
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has 
received or been promised income of $500 or more within 12 months before the 
decision including a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in 
which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, 
a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030{a)). 
Councilmember Devine and Councilmember Najarian each have an interest in GM as a 
business entity. Assuming they receive $500 or more from GM in dividends or other income, in the 
12 months before the decision, they also have interests in GM as a source of income. 
Foreseeability and Materiality: 
At issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions related to the EPC Contract 
will have a material financial effect on the financial interests of either Councilmember Devine or 
Councilmember Najarian. 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the two standards for determining "foreseeability" depending on 
whether an interest is explicitly involved in a decision. Under Regulation 1870I(a), a financial 
interest is explicitly involved where it is a "named party" or the "subject" of a decision. The 
financial interest is the "subject" of a decision where the governmental decision "involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest." (Regulation 18701(a).) 
GE, as a listed and acceptable vendor, will not be named in the proposals responsive to the 
RFP. None of the vendors selected by the EPC Contractor will have rights, duties or entitlements 
under the terms of the submitted bids or the EPC Contract. While Councilmembers will be aware 
that GE has the potential to be selected by the EPC Contractor, there are no facts indicating any 
additional knowledge or surety as to vendors used during the performance of the EPC Contract. 
Moreover, GE is an approved vendor for only a small portion of the contract. There is at least one 
other approved vendor within each category of equipment. And, the EPC Contractor is not limited 


File No. A-17-260 
Page No. 4 
to approved vendors if an alternative vendor is approved by the City. Under the facts provided, GE 
is not explicitly involved in the RFP or EPC Contract decisions. 
Where a financial interest is not explicitly involved, Regulation 18701(b) provides that a 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable where it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and 
more than hypothetical or theoretical . . .  absent extraordinary circumstances beyond the public 
official's control." Under this standard, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered 
reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standard for business entities. 3 For these facts, 
the applicable standard to determine materiality is set forth in Regulation 18702.1 (b ).4 Under that 
standard, materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price 
of the business entity's publicly traded stock." Applicable to these facts, Regulation 18702.l(b) 
provides the following types of decisions that may meet the materiality standard: 
(I) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity 
in which the business entity is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business 
entity supplies; 
GE's potential role as a vendor in the EPC Contract does not appear to meet this standard. 
Given the large value of GE's outstanding shares and its stature as a conglomerate multinational 
corporation, compared to the relatively miniscule nature of any possible change in GE's value 
resultant from GE's potential to be a vendor on the EPC Contract, it is not foreseeable that these 
decisions would contribute to a change in GE's stock value. Councilmembers Devine and Narjarian 
do not have a conflict of interest in participating in decisions related to the RFP or the subsequent 
EPC Contract. 
Section 1090 
Section I 090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies even when 
the terms of the proposed contract are demonstrably fair and equitable, or are plainly to the public 
3 Where the source of income is a business entity, the materiality standard for a business entity in Regulation 
18702. l applies. (Regulation l 8702.3(a)(4 ).) 
4 We note that Regulation 18702. l(a)(4) is not applicable. Under this regulation, a foreseeable effect on a 
business entity is material if the business entity, "ls a named manufacturer in a purchase order of any product purchased 
by the official's agency or the sales provider of any products to the official's agency that aggregates to $1,000 or more 
in any 12-month period," However, as we have detennined that GE is not explicitly involved in the decision, we 
similarly find that GE is not the named manufacture or sales provider under the facts provided. 


File No. A-17-260 
Page No. 5 
entity's advantage. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) Section 1090 is intended "not 
only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of 
Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d I 9 I, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is 
void. (Thomson, supra, at p. 646.) 
As a general rule, when Section l 090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 
647-649; Stigall v. City o/Taft, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987).) However, the Legislature has created various statutory 
exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition, including where the financial interest involved is deemed 
to be a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
Section 1091.S(a) states that an officer's ownership of a small portion of a corporation's 
outstanding shares of stock is a noninterest if the interest is: 
( 1) The ownership of less than 3 percent of the shares of a corporation for profit, 
provided that the total annual income to him or her from dividends, including the 
value of stock dividends, from the corporation does not exceed 5 percent of his or 
her total annual income, and any other payments made to him or her by the 
corporation do not exceed 5 percent of his or her total annual income. 
You indicate that Councilmember Devine owns less than 3 percent of the outstanding shares 
of GE stock, and that her annual income from dividends from GE, or from any other payment by 
GE, does not exceed 5 percent of her total annual income. Similarly, Councilmember Najarian owns 
less than 3 percent of outstanding GE stock shares, and neither his income from dividends from GE, 
nor his income from any other payment from GE, exceeds 5 percent of his total annual income. 
Because Councilmembers Devine and Najarian each have a "noninterest" in their respective GE 
stock under Section 1091.S(a)(l ), Section 1090 does not bar them from making or participating in 
the making of the EPC contract, or the City from entering the EPC contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

plant project, where GE is listed as an approved vendor of equipment in the City's request for 
proposals? 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. 2, unless 
otherwise indicated.
sections.conclusion:
No. Under the Act, neither Councilmember has a conflict of interest in participating in the 
decisions. Further, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmembers from making or 
participating in making, or the City from entering into, the contract because both the 
Councilmembers' respective ownership interests in GE stock qualify for the noninterest exception 
set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(I).

otherwise indicated.
